64da5c1d70e51dc8b7735dd3.question: One story by this author ends with two characters hearing the “rapid discharge of pistol-shots” and knowing it meant that the musicians were announcing that the title event had ended. In that story, one character flirts with another by trying to steal the gold ring from her finger, but she clenches her hand tight. In a story by this author, one character asks if another remembers Assumption, after which they have sex during the title event. Those characters are named Alcée and (*) Calixta. This author described a woman that repeats “body and soul free!” in one story, while in another, Mrs Sommers spends fifteen dollars on frivolous purchases. This author wrote a story in which Armand Aubigny kicks out his wife and child because of their mixed race heritage. For 10 points, name this author of stories such as “The Story of an Hour” and “Désirée’s Baby.”
64da5c1d70e51dc8b7735dd3.question_sanitized: One story by this author ends with two characters hearing the "rapid discharge of pistol-shots" and knowing it meant that the musicians were announcing that the title event had ended. This author described a woman that repeats "body and soul free!" in one story, while in another, Mrs Sommers spends fifteen dollars on frivolous purchases. This author wrote a story in which Armand Aubigny kicks out his wife and child because of their mixed race heritage. For 10 points, name this author of stories such as "The Story of an Hour" and "Desiree's Baby."
64da5c1d70e51dc8b7735dd3.answer: Kate Chopin (The unnamed stories are, in order, “At the ‘Cadian Ball,” “The Storm,” and “A Pair of Silk Stockings.”)

64da5c1d70e51dc8b7735dd7.question: One poem by this man ends by saying “where there was / just barely a hut to receive it … with an entrance whose gatepost trembled- / there you crafted a temple for their hearing.” A collection by this author includes sections titled The Book of Monastic Life and The Book of Poverty and Death. The image of an “emotion / that almost dismays us / when a joyful thing falls” ends a poem by this author that describes Lament leading a boy to the (*) Mountains of Primal Grief. This author opened one collection with the line “There rose a Tree. O pure transcendence!” and asked “have you remembered / Gaspara Stampa sufficiently yet?” in another work. This author’s most famous collection proclaims “Every Angel is terror” and opens by asking “Who, if I cried out, would hear me among the Angelic / Orders?” For 10 points, name this German poet of the Duino Elegies.

This author opened one collection with the line "There rose a Tree. O pure transcendence!" and asked "have you remembered / Gaspara Stampa sufficiently yet?" in another work. This author's most famous collection proclaims "Every Angel is terror" and opens by asking "Who, if I cried out, would hear me among the Angelic / Orders?" For 10 points, name this German poet of the Duino Elegies.
64da5c1d70e51dc8b7735dd7.answer: Rainer Maria Rilke (The first and fifth line refers to Sonnets to Orpheus ; the second line refers to the Book of Hours )

A central character in one story by this author first regrets bringing a slice of bread with butter outside, but eventually takes a big bite. A poem in one story by this author begins with the line (*) "Why must it always be tomato soup?" That poem features in a story by this author that describes a pear tree that stretches up "like the flame of a candle." In another story by this author, one character brings cream puffs to comfort the family of the dead Mr. Scott and asks "Isn't life-?" That story features Laura Sheridan as she sets up the title event. For 10 points, what author featured rich descriptions of food in stories such as "Bliss" and "The Garden Party?"
64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e16.answer: Katherine Mansfield (The story in the first line is “Sun and Moon.”)

64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e1c.question: A poem titled for a landmark in this place declares “I am / the perfect man / the Buddha of this World.” A poem that opens “How many dawns, chill from his rippling rest / The seagull’s wings shall dip and pivot him” and asks a landmark in this place to “sometime sweep, descend / And of the curveship lend a myth to God.” This place titles a poem that describes the “similitudes of the past and those of the future” and asks (*) “what is the count of the scores or hundreds of years between us?” A poem titled for this place describes “crowds of men and women attired in the usual costumes” and declares “you furnish your parts towards the soul.” A landmark in this place inspired a modernist epic by Hart Crane. For ten points, Walt Whitman declared “flood-tide below me! I watch you face to face” in a poem titled for a ferry trip to what place?
64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e1c.question_sanitized: A poem titled for a landmark in this place declares "I am / the perfect man / the Buddha of this World." A poem that opens "How many dawns, chill from his rippling rest / The seagull's wings shall dip and pivot him" and asks a landmark in this place to "sometime sweep, descend / And of the curveship lend a myth to God." This place titles a poem that describes the "similitudes of the past and those of the future" and asks (*) "what is the count of the scores or hundreds of years between us?" A poem titled for this place describes "crowds of men and women attired in the usual costumes" and declares "you furnish your parts towards the soul." A landmark in this place inspired a modernist epic by Hart Crane. For ten points, Walt Whitman declared "flood-tide below me! I watch you face to face" in a poem titled for a ferry trip to what place?
64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e1c.answer: Brooklyn [accept the Brooklyn Bridge] (The clued poems are Jack Keroac’s “Brooklyn Bridge Blues,” Hart Crane’s “Proem: To Brooklyn Bridge;” and Whitman’s “Crossing Brooklyn Ferry.”)

64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e23.question: A poet from this country addressed a creature as a “brief rainbow, iris, twin to the glance mounted within your lashes” and “love’s arrow on earth”. An author from this country described how “sinking is sweet in such a sea” in a 15-line sonnet addressed to infinity. A poet from this country contrasted “laureate poets” who “seem to wander” while “even the poor / know that richness” of the title plants. This country was home to the (*) hermeticist writer of the collection Cuttlefish Bones. The Earl of Surrey translated a work from this country that describes “the sun’s rays turned pale with grief” in a collection about a woman he met on Good Friday. An author from this country addressed “you who hear the sound in scattered rhymes” in a collection dedicated to Laura. For 10 points, name this home country of Eugenio Montale and Petrarch.
64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e23.question_sanitized: A poet from this country addressed a creature as a "brief rainbow, iris, twin to the glance mounted within your lashes" and "love's arrow on earth". A poet from this country contrasted "laureate poets" who "seem to wander" while "even the poor / know that richness" of the title plants. This country was home to the (*) hermeticist writer of the collection Cuttlefish Bones. The Earl of Surrey translated a work from this country that describes "the sun's rays turned pale with grief" in a collection about a woman he met on Good Friday. An author from this country addressed "you who hear the sound in scattered rhymes" in a collection dedicated to Laura. For 10 points, name this home country of Eugenio Montale and Petrarch.
64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e23.answer: Italy [accept Italia ] (The first line refers to Eugenio Montale’s “The Eel. “The Infinite” is by Giacomo Leopardi.”)
64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e23.answer_sanitized: Italy [accept  Italia ] (The first line refers to Eugenio Montale's "The Eel. "The Infinite" is by Giacomo Leopardi.")

64da5c1e70e51dc8b7735e34.question: In The Narrow Road to the Deep North , a haiku that describes “A thick of summer grass” is preceded by a paraphrase of one poem by this author. That poem by this author describes “white hair scratched even shorter- / Soon too few to hold a hairpin on” and opens “the country shattered, mountains and rivers remain.” This man asked “what time will the wild goose come?” in a poem titled “Thinking of [this man’s friend] at the End of the Sky.” One poem by this author declares that “having girls is best,” because “Our sons are merely buried amid the grass.” Another poem by this author describes how the (*) autumn wind “cries out in rage” and ruins his “little hut” and “Thatched Roof.” This man opened one poem by saying “The wagons rumble and roll,/ The horses whinny and neigh;” that poem is titled “Ballad of the Army Carts.” For 10 points, name this Tang Dynasty poet and friend of Li Bai.

64da5c1f70e51dc8b7735e49.question: One poem in this language declares “this is the end of human beauty.” The speaker of a poem in this language pleads “Men by brothers who after us live / have your hearts against us not hardened” and repeats “pray God would us all absolve.” A poem in this language asks where “Lady Flora the lovely Roman” is. In an epic written in this language, a king fights the emir of Babylon and is later ordered by Gabriel to free a city from a siege. (*) Dante Gabriel Rosetti’s translation of a poem in this language titled “The Ballad of the Dead Ladies” contains the famous line “But where are the snows of yesteryear?” In one epic poem written in this language, the title character dies while blowing his oliphaunt horn after being betrayed by Ganelon. For 10 points, name this language used to write The Song of Roland.
64da5c1f70e51dc8b7735e49.question_sanitized: One poem in this language declares "this is the end of human beauty." For 10 points, name this language used to write The Song of Roland.
64da5c1f70e51dc8b7735e49.answer: French (The first line refers to “Les Regrets De La Belle Heaulmière,” which inspired a Rodin sculpture. The second line is from “The Ballad of the Hanged Men” and is part of the epigraph to In Cold Blood .”)
